input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project that multi-targets both frameworks to compare them.
data.reasoningText:
Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that benchmarks the three methods (AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, UniqueWordCount) and runs against both net9.0 and net10.0 target frameworks.

Let me create a benchmark project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0 and uses BenchmarkDotNet.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-6c059230b8444f1294f31f8042331e77/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-6c059230b8444f1294f31f8042331e77/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-6c059230b8444f1294f31f8042331e77/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />

+</Project>

data.reasoningText:
The version of BenchmarkDotNet doesn't have `CoreRuntime.Core100` yet. Let me check what's available, or I can use a different approach - specify runtimes via attributes or use the `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion` method.

Let me use `RuntimeMoniker` or create a custom runtime. Actually, I can use `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("net10.0", ".NET 10.0")` or just use the moniker approach.

Actually, I think the simplest approach is to use `Job.ShortRun.WithRuntime(CoreRuntime.Core90)` and `Job.ShortRun.WithRuntime(new CoreRuntime(RuntimeMoniker.NotRecognized, "net10.0", ".NET 10"))` or construct it manually.


Looking at the BenchmarkDotNet API, I need to pass a `RuntimeMoniker`, an msBuild moniker string, and a display name to the `CoreRuntime` constructor. I could try using `RuntimeMoniker.Net100` if it exists, or fall back to a simpler approach like defining jobs through attributes instead.

Actually, the most straightforward solution is just to pass the runtimes directly via the CLI with `--runtimes net9.0 net10.0`.
